title: Add asset manifest schema helpers
description: Implement onboarding helpers to build, read, and write `.takt/assets-manifest.json`, including SHA-256 hashing, takt version capture, install timestamp, and tracked asset entry structure for bundled assets.
acceptance_criteria: `write_assets_manifest(project_root, installed_files)` writes manifest JSON with `takt_version`, `installed_at`, and per-asset `sha256`, `source`, and `user_owned` fields.
`read_assets_manifest(project_root)` returns parsed manifest data and safely returns an empty structure when the manifest is absent.
Manifest helper logic tracks only the bundled asset roots in scope for this feature and leaves always-user-owned paths out of manifest generation.

handoff_summary.completed:
- `write_assets_manifest(project_root, installed_files)` — computes SHA-256 per file, filters to bundled asset roots only, writes JSON manifest with `takt_version`, `installed_at`, and per-asset entries
- `read_assets_manifest(project_root)` — parses manifest JSON; returns empty structure on missing file or parse error
- Private helpers: `_sha256_file`, `_is_user_owned`, `_empty_manifest`
- Module-level constants: `_MANIFEST_FILENAME`, `_BUNDLED_ASSET_PREFIXES`, `_USER_OWNED_PREFIXES`
- Committed to feature branch `feature/b-af3f17ad`
handoff_summary.remaining: Downstream beads will need to: (1) call `write_assets_manifest` at the end of `scaffold_project()`, (2) implement the `takt upgrade` command in `cli.py`, (3) implement `merge_config_keys()` for config key-merge, and (4) implement `takt asset` subcommands.
handoff_summary.risks:
- `installed_files` callers must pass absolute paths within `project_root`; out-of-tree paths are silently skipped — this is intentional but callers should be aware
- `_pkg_version("agent-takt")` will raise `PackageNotFoundError` in uninstalled dev environments where the package metadata is absent; tester should verify behavior in editable-install context
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester should verify write_assets_manifest produces correct JSON structure, SHA-256 values match file contents, templates are user_owned=true, non-bundled paths are excluded, and read_assets_manifest returns empty structure when manifest is absent.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Low — only `src/agent_takt/onboarding.py` touched. Sibling beads working on `cli.py` or other files in this feature tree are unaffected.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: 1. **`installed_files` as a flat list of absolute Paths**: Callers (e.g. `scaffold_project`) already collect written paths as lists; this avoids a bespoke dict parameter and keeps the interface consistent with `install_templates_with_substitution` return value. 2. **Filtering to `_BUNDLED_ASSET_PREFIXES` inside the function**: The function self-scopes to only bundled asset roots rather than trusting callers to pass the right subset. This prevents `docs/memory/` or `specs/` files from accidentally entering the manifest if callers pass a broader list. 3. **`user_owned: true` for templates at init time**: Per spec resolved decision — templates are installed with placeholder substitution, so the on-disk sha would never match the bundled sha; the upgrade command would always see them as locally modified. Flagging them `user_owned` avoids a permanent false-positive. 4. **`read_assets_manifest` returns empty dict on any error**: OSError and JSONDecodeError both return the same empty structure so callers do not need to distinguish failure modes. The takt upgrade logic should treat an empty manifest the same as a fresh install.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: 1. `write_assets_manifest` — verify JSON structure keys (`takt_version`, `installed_at`, `assets`), that SHA-256 values match actual file contents, that `templates/agents/` files have `user_owned: true` and `source: "bundled"`, that `.agents/skills/` files have `user_owned: false`, and that files under `docs/memory/` are excluded even if passed in `installed_files`. 2. `read_assets_manifest` with absent file — must return `{"takt_version": "", "installed_at": "", "assets": {}}`. 3. `read_assets_manifest` with malformed JSON — must return empty structure rather than raising. 4. Round-trip: write then read should return equivalent data. 5. Verify `_MANIFEST_FILENAME` resolves to `.takt/assets-manifest.json` relative to project root.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: - `merge_config_keys()` described in the spec is not implemented here — it belongs to a separate bead or a future extension of this bead. - `write_assets_manifest` does not currently handle updating an existing manifest (merging new entries); it overwrites unconditionally. The upgrade logic in `cli.py` will manage partial updates using `read_assets_manifest` + selective overwrite. - The `.takt/config.yaml` path matching uses `rel_str == prefix.rstrip("/")` because it is a file path, not a directory prefix; this is correct but relies on the caller passing the config file explicitly in `installed_files`.

conflict_risks: Low — only `src/agent_takt/onboarding.py` touched. Sibling beads working on `cli.py` or other files in this feature tree are unaffected.

execution_history.summary: Added `write_assets_manifest()` and `read_assets_manifest()` to `src/agent_takt/onboarding.py`. The helpers implement SHA-256 hashing, takt version capture via `importlib.metadata`, UTC install timestamp, and the per-asset `sha256`/`source`/`user_owned` entry structure. Only bundled asset roots (`templates/agents/`, `.agents/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, `.takt/config.yaml`) are tracked; `docs/memory/`, `specs/`, and `CLAUDE.md` are excluded. Guardrail templates are flagged `user_owned: true` at init time because placeholder substitution produces on-disk content that differs from the bundled source. `read_assets_manifest` returns an empty `{"takt_version": "", "installed_at": "", "assets": {}}` structure when the manifest is absent or unreadable.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
